    73→131-## Configuration
    74→132-
    75→133-Hooks are defined in JSON settings files. The configuration has three levels of nesting:
    76→134-
    77→135-1. Choose a [hook event](#hook-events) to respond to, like `PreToolUse` or `Stop`
    78→136:2. Add a [matcher group](#matcher-patterns) to filter when it fires, like "only for the Bash tool"
    79→137-3. Define one or more [hook handlers](#hook-handler-fields) to run when matched
    80→138-
    81→139-See [How a hook resolves](#how-a-hook-resolves) above for a complete walkthrough with an annotated example.
    82→140-
    83→141-<Note>
    84→142-  This page uses specific terms for each level: **hook event** for the lifecycle point, **matcher group** for the filter, and **hook handler** for the shell command, prompt, or agent that runs. "Hook" on its own refers to the general feature.
    85→143-</Note>
    86→144-
    87→145-### Hook locations
    88→146-
    89→--
    90→156-| [Skill](/en/skills) or [agent](/en/sub-agents) frontmatter | While the component is active | Yes, defined in the component file |
    91→157-
    92→158-For details on settings file resolution, see [settings](/en/settings). Enterprise administrators can use `allowManagedHooksOnly` to block user, project, and plugin hooks. See [Hook configuration](/en/settings#hook-configuration).
    93→159-
    94→160-### Matcher patterns
    95→161-
    96→162-The `matcher` field is a regex string that filters when hooks fire. Use `"*"`, `""`, or omit `matcher` entirely to match all occurrences. Each event type matches on a different field:
    97→163-
    98→164-| Event                                                                                           | What the matcher filters  | Example matcher values                                                             |
    99→165-| :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------ | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   100→166:| `PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `PostToolUseFailure`, `PermissionRequest`                          | tool name                 | `Bash`, `Edit\|Write`, `mcp__.*`                                                   |
   101→167-| `SessionStart`                                                                                  | how the session started   | `startup`, `resume`, `clear`, `compact`                                            |
   102→168-| `SessionEnd`                                                                                    | why the session ended     | `clear`, `logout`, `prompt_input_exit`, `bypass_permissions_disabled`, `other`     |
   103→169-| `Notification`                                                                                  | notification type         | `permission_prompt`, `idle_prompt`, `auth_success`, `elicitation_dialog`           |
   104→170:| `SubagentStart`                                                                                 | agent type                | `Bash`, `Explore`, `Plan`, or custom agent names                                   |
   105→171-| `PreCompact`                                                                                    | what triggered compaction | `manual`, `auto`                                                                   |
   106→172-| `SubagentStop`                                                                                  | agent type                | same values as `SubagentStart`                                                     |
   107→173-| `ConfigChange`                                                                                  | configuration source      | `user_settings`, `project_settings`, `local_settings`, `policy_settings`, `skills` |
   108→174-| `UserPromptSubmit`, `Stop`, `TeammateIdle`, `TaskCompleted`, `WorktreeCreate`, `WorktreeRemove` | no matcher support        | always fires on every occurrence                                                   |
   109→175-
   110→176:The matcher is a regex, so `Edit|Write` matches either tool and `Notebook.*` matches any tool starting with Notebook. The matcher runs against a field from the [JSON input](#hook-input-and-output) that Claude Code sends to your hook on stdin. For tool events, that field is `tool_name`. Each [hook event](#hook-events) section lists the full set of matcher values and the input schema for that event.
   111→177-

   114→180-```json  theme={null}
   115→181-{
   116→182-  "hooks": {
   117→183-    "PostToolUse": [
   118→184-      {
   119→185:        "matcher": "Edit|Write",
   120→186-        "hooks": [
   121→187-          {
   122→188-            "type": "command",
   123→189-            "command": "/path/to/lint-check.sh"
   124→190-          }
   125→191-        ]
   126→192-      }
   127→193-    ]
   128→194-  }
   129→195-}
   130→--
   131→240-  }
   132→241-}

   134→243-
   135→244-### Hook handler fields
   136→245-
   137→246-Each object in the inner `hooks` array is a hook handler: the shell command, LLM prompt, or agent that runs when the matcher matches. There are three types:
   138→247-
   139→248-* **[Command hooks](#command-hook-fields)** (`type: "command"`): run a shell command. Your script receives the event's [JSON input](#hook-input-and-output) on stdin and communicates results back through exit codes and stdout.
   140→249-* **[Prompt hooks](#prompt-and-agent-hook-fields)** (`type: "prompt"`): send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation. The model returns a yes/no decision as JSON. See [Prompt-based hooks](#prompt-based-hooks).
   141→250:* **[Agent hooks](#prompt-and-agent-hook-fields)** (`type: "agent"`): spawn a subagent that can use tools like Read, Grep, and Glob to verify conditions before returning a decision. See [Agent-based hooks](#agent-based-hooks).
   142→251-
   143→252-#### Common fields
   144→253-
   145→254-These fields apply to all hook types:
   146→255-
   147→256-| Field           | Required | Description                                                                                                                                   |
   148→257-| :-------------- | :------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   149→258-| `type`          | yes      | `"command"`, `"prompt"`, or `"agent"`                                                                                                         |
   150→259-| `timeout`       | no       | Seconds before canceling. Defaults: 600 for command, 30 for prompt, 60 for agent                                                              |
   151→260-| `statusMessage` | no       | Custom spinner message displayed while the hook runs                                                                                          |
   152→--
   153→282-
   154→283-### Reference scripts by path
   155→284-
   156→285-Use environment variables to reference hook scripts relative to the project or plugin root, regardless of the working directory when the hook runs:
   157→286-
   158→287-* `$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR`: the project root. Wrap in quotes to handle paths with spaces.
   159→288-* `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}`: the plugin's root directory, for scripts bundled with a [plugin](/en/plugins).
   160→289-

69:The script reads the JSON input from stdin, extracts the command, and returns a `permissionDecision` of `"deny"` if it contains `rm -rf`:
70-
71-```bash  theme={null}
72-#!/bin/bash
73-# .claude/hooks/block-rm.sh
74:COMMAND=$(jq -r '.tool_input.command')
75-
76-if echo "$COMMAND" | grep -q 'rm -rf'; then
77-  jq -n '{
78-    hookSpecificOutput: {
79-      hookEventName: "PreToolUse",
80:      permissionDecision: "deny",
81:      permissionDecisionReason: "Destructive command blocked by hook"
82-    }
83-  }'
84-else
85-  exit 0  # allow the command
86-fi
--

100:    { "tool_name": "Bash", "tool_input": { "command": "rm -rf /tmp/build" }, ... }
101-    ```
102-  </Step>
103-
104-  <Step title="Matcher checks">
105-    The matcher `"Bash"` matches the tool name, so `block-rm.sh` runs. If you omit the matcher or use `"*"`, the hook runs on every occurrence of the event. Hooks only skip when a matcher is defined and doesn't match.
--
110-
111-    ```json  theme={null}
112-    {
113-      "hookSpecificOutput": {
114-        "hookEventName": "PreToolUse",
115:        "permissionDecision": "deny",
116:        "permissionDecisionReason": "Destructive command blocked by hook"
117-      }
118-    }
119-    ```
120-
121-    If the command had been safe (like `npm test`), the script would hit `exit 0` instead, which tells Claude Code to allow the tool call with no further action.
--
432-For example, a hook command script that blocks dangerous Bash commands:
433-
434-```bash  theme={null}
435-#!/bin/bash
436-# Reads JSON input from stdin, checks the command
437:command=$(jq -r '.tool_input.command' < /dev/stdin)
438-
439-if [[ "$command" == rm* ]]; then
440-  echo "Blocked: rm commands are not allowed" >&2
441-  exit 2  # Blocking error: tool call is prevented
442-fi
--

504-| Events                                                                              | Decision pattern     | Key fields                                                                  |
505-| :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
506-| UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure, Stop, SubagentStop, ConfigChange | Top-level `decision` | `decision: "block"`, `reason`                                               |
507-| TeammateIdle, TaskCompleted                                                         | Exit code only       | Exit code 2 blocks the action, stderr is fed back as feedback               |
508:| PreToolUse                                                                          | `hookSpecificOutput` | `permissionDecision` (allow/deny/ask), `permissionDecisionReason`           |
509-| PermissionRequest                                                                   | `hookSpecificOutput` | `decision.behavior` (allow/deny)                                            |
510-| WorktreeCreate                                                                      | stdout path          | Hook prints absolute path to created worktree. Non-zero exit fails creation |
511-| WorktreeRemove, Notification, SessionEnd, PreCompact                                | None                 | No decision control. Used for side effects like logging or cleanup          |
512-

# Hooks reference

> Reference for Claude Code hook events, configuration schema, JSON input/output formats, exit codes, async hooks, prompt hooks, and MCP tool hooks.

<Tip>
  For a quickstart guide with examples, see [Automate workflows with hooks](/en/hooks-guide).
</Tip>

Hooks are user-defined shell commands or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Use this reference to look up event schemas, configuration options, JSON input/output formats, and advanced features like async hooks and MCP tool hooks. If you're setting up hooks for the first time, start with the [guide](/en/hooks-guide) instead.

## Hook lifecycle

Hooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session. When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler. For command hooks, this arrives on stdin. Your handler can then inspect the input, take action, and optionally return a decision. Some events fire once per session, while others fire repeatedly inside the agentic loop:

## What the Documentation States

The settings.json documentation mentions:

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    // Configure custom commands to run at lifecycle events
    // See [hooks documentation](/en/hooks) for format
  }
}
```

The page explicitly states:
> **hooks** | Configure custom commands to run at lifecycle events. See [hooks documentation](/en/hooks) for format

## Key Related Information

The documentation does provide these hook-related settings:

| Setting | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `hooks` | Configure custom commands to run at lifecycle events |
| `disableAllHooks` | Disable all hooks and any custom status line |
| `allowManagedHooksOnly` | (Managed settings only) Prevent loading of user, project, and plugin hooks. Only allows managed hooks and SDK hooks |

## Recommendation

To find the **exact JSON structure for hooks, PreToolUse hooks, and their input/output formats**, you need to:

     5→# Hooks reference
     6→

    12→
    13→Hooks are user-defined shell commands or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle. Use this reference to look up event schemas, configuration options, JSON input/output formats, and advanced features like async hooks and MCP tool hooks. If you're setting up hooks for the first time, start with the [guide](/en/hooks-guide) instead.
    14→
    15→## Hook lifecycle
    16→
    17→Hooks fire at specific points during a Claude Code session. When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler. For command hooks, this arrives on stdin. Your handler can then inspect the input, take action, and optionally return a decision. Some events fire once per session, while others fire repeatedly inside the agentic loop:

    16→47-    47→### How a hook resolves
    17→48-    48→
    18→49:    49→To see how these pieces fit together, consider this `PreToolUse` hook that blocks destructive shell commands. The hook runs `block-rm.sh` before every Bash tool call:
    19→50-    50→
    20→51-    51→```json  theme={null}
    21→52-    52→{
    22→53-    53→  "hooks": {
    23→54:    54→    "PreToolUse": [
    24→55-    55→      {
    25→56-    56→        "matcher": "Bash",
    26→57-    57→        "hooks": [
    27→58-    58→          {
    28→59-    59→            "type": "command",
    29→--
    30→74-    74→COMMAND=$(jq -r '.tool_input.command')
    31→75-    75→
    32→76-    76→if echo "$COMMAND" | grep -q 'rm -rf'; then
    33→77-    77→  jq -n '{
    34→78-    78→    hookSpecificOutput: {
    35→79:    79→      hookEventName: "PreToolUse",
    36→80-    80→      permissionDecision: "deny",
    37→81-    81→      permissionDecisionReason: "Destructive command blocked by hook"
    38→82-    82→    }
    39→83-    83→  }'
    40→84-    84→else
    41→--
    42→87-    87→```
    43→88-    88→
    44→89-    89→Now suppose Claude Code decides to run `Bash "rm -rf /tmp/build"`. Here's what happens:
    45→90-    90→
    46→91-    91→<Frame>
    47→92:[Omitted long matching line]
    48→93-    93→</Frame>
    49→94-    94→
    50→95-    95→<Steps>
    51→96-    96→  <Step title="Event fires">
    52→97:    97→    The `PreToolUse` event fires. Claude Code sends the tool input as JSON on stdin to the hook:
    53→98-    98→
    54→99-    99→    ```json  theme={null}
    55→100-   100→    { "tool_name": "Bash", "tool_input": { "command": "rm -rf /tmp/build" }, ... }
    56→101-   101→    ```
    57→102-   102→  </Step>
    58→--
    59→109-   109→    The script extracts `"rm -rf /tmp/build"` from the input and finds `rm -rf`, so it prints a decision to stdout:
    60→110-   110→
    61→111-   111→    ```json  theme={null}
    62→112-   112→    {
    63→113-   113→      "hookSpecificOutput": {
    64→114:   114→        "hookEventName": "PreToolUse",
    65→115-   115→        "permissionDecision": "deny",
    66→116-   116→        "permissionDecisionReason": "Destructive command blocked by hook"
    67→117-   117→      }
    68→118-   118→    }
    69→119-   119→    ```
    70→--
    71→130-   130→
    72→131-   131→## Configuration
    73→132-   132→
    74→133-   133→Hooks are defined in JSON settings files. The configuration has three levels of nesting:
    75→134-   134→
    76→135:   135→1. Choose a [hook event](#hook-events) to respond to, like `PreToolUse` or `Stop`
    77→136-   136→2. Add a [matcher group](#matcher-patterns) to filter when it fires, like "only for the Bash tool"
    78→137-   137→3. Define one or more [hook handlers](#hook-handler-fields) to run when matched
    79→138-   138→
    80→139-   139→See [How a hook resolves](#how-a-hook-resolves) above for a complete walkthrough with an annotated example.
    81→140-   140→
    82→--
    83→146-   146→
    84→147-   147→Where you define a hook determines its scope:
    85→148-   148→
    86→149-   149→| Location                                                   | Scope                         | Shareable                          |
    87→150-   150→| :--------------------------------------------------------- | :---------------------------- | :--------------------------------- |
    88→151:   151→| `~/.claude/settings.json`                                  | All your projects             | No, local to your machine          |
    89→152:   152→| `.claude/settings.json`                                    | Single project                | Yes, can be committed to the repo  |
    90→153-   153→| `.claude/settings.local.json`                              | Single project                | No, gitignored                     |
    91→154-   154→| Managed policy settings                                    | Organization-wide             | Yes, admin-controlled              |
    92→155-   155→| [Plugin](/en/plugins) `hooks/hooks.json`                   | When plugin is enabled        | Yes, bundled with the plugin       |
    93→156-   156→| [Skill](/en/skills) or [agent](/en/sub-agents) frontmatter | While the component is active | Yes, defined in the component file |
    94→157-   157→
    95→--
    96→161-   161→
    97→162-   162→The `matcher` field is a regex string that filters when hooks fire. Use `"*"`, `""`, or omit `matcher` entirely to match all occurrences. Each event type matches on a different field:
    98→163-   163→
    99→164-   164→| Event                                                                                           | What the matcher filters  | Example matcher values                                                             |
   100→165-   165→| :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------------ | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   101→166:   166→| `PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `PostToolUseFailure`, `PermissionRequest`                          | tool name                 | `Bash`, `Edit\|Write`, `mcp__.*`                                                   |
   102→167-   167→| `SessionStart`                                                                                  | how the session started   | `startup`, `resume`, `clear`, `compact`                                            |
   103→168-   168→| `SessionEnd`                                                                                    | why the session ended     | `clear`, `logout`, `prompt_input_exit`, `bypass_permissions_disabled`, `other`     |
   104→169-   169→| `Notification`                                                                                  | notification type         | `permission_prompt`, `idle_prompt`, `auth_success`, `elicitation_dialog`           |
   105→170-   170→| `SubagentStart`                                                                                 | agent type                | `Bash`, `Explore`, `Plan`, or custom agent names                                   |
   106→171-   171→| `PreCompact`                                                                                    | what triggered compaction | `manual`, `auto`                                                                   |
   107→--
   108→197-   197→
   109→198-   198→`UserPromptSubmit`, `Stop`, `TeammateIdle`, `TaskCompleted`, `WorktreeCreate`, and `WorktreeRemove` don't support matchers and always fire on every occurrence. If you add a `matcher` field to these events, it is silently ignored.
   110→199-   199→
   111→200-   200→#### Match MCP tools
   112→201-   201→
   113→202:   202→[MCP](/en/mcp) server tools appear as regular tools in tool events (`PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `PostToolUseFailure`, `PermissionRequest`), so you can match them the same way you match any other tool name.
   114→203-   203→
   115→204-   204→MCP tools follow the naming pattern `mcp__<server>__<tool>`, for example:
   116→205-   205→
   117→206-   206→* `mcp__memory__create_entities`: Memory server's create entities tool
   118→207-   207→* `mcp__filesystem__read_file`: Filesystem server's read file tool
   119→--
   120→215-   215→This example logs all memory server operations and validates write operations from any MCP server:
   121→216-   216→
   122→217-   217→```json  theme={null}
   123→218-   218→{
   124→219-   219→  "hooks": {
   125→220:   220→    "PreToolUse": [
   126→221-   221→      {
   127→222-   222→        "matcher": "mcp__memory__.*",
   128→223-   223→        "hooks": [
   129→224-   224→          {
   130→225-   225→            "type": "command",
   131→--
   132→345-   345→
   133→346-   346→All hook events are supported. For subagents, `Stop` hooks are automatically converted to `SubagentStop` since that is the event that fires when a subagent completes.
   134→347-   347→
   135→348-   348→Hooks use the same configuration format as settings-based hooks but are scoped to the component's lifetime and cleaned up when it finishes.
   136→349-   349→
   137→350:   350→This skill defines a `PreToolUse` hook that runs a security validation script before each `Bash` command:
   138→351-   351→
   139→352-   352→```yaml  theme={null}
   140→353-   353→---
   141→354-   354→name: secure-operations
   142→355-   355→description: Perform operations with security checks
   143→356-   356→hooks:
   144→357:   357→  PreToolUse:
   145→358-   358→    - matcher: "Bash"
   146→359-   359→      hooks:
   147→360-   360→        - type: command
   148→361-   361→          command: "./scripts/security-check.sh"
   149→362-   362→---
   150→--
   151→368-   368→
   152→369-   369→Type `/hooks` in Claude Code to open the interactive hooks manager, where you can view, add, and delete hooks without editing settings files directly. For a step-by-step walkthrough, see [Set up your first hook](/en/hooks-guide#set-up-your-first-hook) in the guide.
   153→370-   370→
   154→371-   371→Each hook in the menu is labeled with a bracket prefix indicating its source:
   155→372-   372→
   156→373:   373→* `[User]`: from `~/.claude/settings.json`
   157→374:   374→* `[Project]`: from `.claude/settings.json`
   158→375-   375→* `[Local]`: from `.claude/settings.local.json`
   159→376-   376→* `[Plugin]`: from a plugin's `hooks/hooks.json`, read-only
   160→377-   377→
   161→378-   378→### Disable or remove hooks
   162→379-   379→
   163→--
   164→383-   383→
   165→384-   384→The `disableAllHooks` setting respects the managed settings hierarchy. If an administrator has configured hooks through managed policy settings, `disableAllHooks` set in user, project, or local settings cannot disable those managed hooks. Only `disableAllHooks` set at the managed settings level can disable managed hooks.
   166→385-   385→
   167→386-   386→Direct edits to hooks in settings files don't take effect immediately. Claude Code captures a snapshot of hooks at startup and uses it throughout the session. This prevents malicious or accidental hook modifications from taking effect mid-session without your review. If hooks are modified externally, Claude Code warns you and requires review in the `/hooks` menu before changes apply.
   168→387-   387→
   169→388:   388→## Hook input and output
   170→389-   389→
   171→390-   390→Hooks receive JSON data via stdin and communicate results through exit codes, stdout, and stderr. This section covers fields and behavior common to all events. Each event's section under [Hook events](#hook-events) includes its specific input schema and decision control options.
   172→391-   391→
   173→392-   392→### Common input fields
   174→393-   393→
   175→--
   176→399-   399→| `transcript_path` | Path to conversation JSON                                                                                                                  |
   177→400-   400→| `cwd`             | Current working directory when the hook is invoked                                                                                         |
   178→401-   401→| `permission_mode` | Current [permission mode](/en/permissions#permission-modes): `"default"`, `"plan"`, `"acceptEdits"`, `"dontAsk"`, or `"bypassPermissions"` |
   179→402-   402→| `hook_event_name` | Name of the event that fired                                                                                                               |
   180→403-   403→
   181→404:   404→For example, a `PreToolUse` hook for a Bash command receives this on stdin:
   182→405-   405→
   183→406-   406→```json  theme={null}
   184→407-   407→{
   185→408-   408→  "session_id": "abc123",
   186→409-   409→  "transcript_path": "/home/user/.claude/projects/.../transcript.jsonl",
   187→410-   410→  "cwd": "/home/user/my-project",
   188→411-   411→  "permission_mode": "default",
   189→412:   412→  "hook_event_name": "PreToolUse",
   190→413-   413→  "tool_name": "Bash",
   191→414-   414→  "tool_input": {
   192→415-   415→    "command": "npm test"
   193→416-   416→  }
   194→417-   417→}
   195→--
   196→423-   423→
   197→424-   424→The exit code from your hook command tells Claude Code whether the action should proceed, be blocked, or be ignored.
   198→425-   425→
   199→426-   426→**Exit 0** means success. Claude Code parses stdout for [JSON output fields](#json-output). JSON output is only processed on exit 0. For most events, stdout is only shown in verbose mode (`Ctrl+O`). The exceptions are `UserPromptSubmit` and `SessionStart`, where stdout is added as context that Claude can see and act on.
   200→427-   427→
   201→428:   428→**Exit 2** means a blocking error. Claude Code ignores stdout and any JSON in it. Instead, stderr text is fed back to Claude as an error message. The effect depends on the event: `PreToolUse` blocks the tool call, `UserPromptSubmit` rejects the prompt, and so on. See [exit code 2 behavior](#exit-code-2-behavior-per-event) for the full list.
   202→429-   429→
   203→430-   430→**Any other exit code** is a non-blocking error. stderr is shown in verbose mode (`Ctrl+O`) and execution continues.
   204→431-   431→
   205→432-   432→For example, a hook command script that blocks dangerous Bash commands:
   206→433-   433→
   207→--
   208→448-   448→
   209→449-   449→Exit code 2 is the way a hook signals "stop, don't do this." The effect depends on the event, because some events represent actions that can be blocked (like a tool call that hasn't happened yet) and others represent things that already happened or can't be prevented.
   210→450-   450→
   211→451-   451→| Hook event           | Can block? | What happens on exit 2                                                        |
   212→452-   452→| :------------------- | :--------- | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   213→453:   453→| `PreToolUse`         | Yes        | Blocks the tool call                                                          |
   214→454-   454→| `PermissionRequest`  | Yes        | Denies the permission                                                         |
   215→455-   455→| `UserPromptSubmit`   | Yes        | Blocks prompt processing and erases the prompt                                |
   216→456-   456→| `Stop`               | Yes        | Prevents Claude from stopping, continues the conversation                     |
   217→457-   457→| `SubagentStop`       | Yes        | Prevents the subagent from stopping                                           |
   218→458-   458→| `TeammateIdle`       | Yes        | Prevents the teammate from going idle (teammate continues working)            |
   219→--
   220→503-   503→
   221→504-   504→| Events                                                                              | Decision pattern     | Key fields                                                                  |
   222→505-   505→| :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   223→506-   506→| UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure, Stop, SubagentStop, ConfigChange | Top-level `decision` | `decision: "block"`, `reason`                                               |
   224→507-   507→| TeammateIdle, TaskCompleted                                                         | Exit code only       | Exit code 2 blocks the action, stderr is fed back as feedback               |
   225→508:   508→| PreToolUse                                                                          | `hookSpecificOutput` | `permissionDecision` (allow/deny/ask), `permissionDecisionReason`           |
   226→509-   509→| PermissionRequest                                                                   | `hookSpecificOutput` | `decision.behavior` (allow/deny)                                            |
   227→510-   510→| WorktreeCreate                                                                      | stdout path          | Hook prints absolute path to created worktree. Non-zero exit fails creation |
   228→511-   511→| WorktreeRemove, Notification, SessionEnd, PreCompact                                | None                 | No decision control. Used for side effects like logging or cleanup          |
   229→512-   512→
   230→513-   513→Here are examples of each pattern in action:
   231→--
   232→522-   522→      "reason": "Test suite must pass before proceeding"
   233→523-   523→    }
   234→524-   524→    ```
   235→525-   525→  </Tab>
   236→526-   526→
   237→527:   527→  <Tab title="PreToolUse">
   238→528:   528→    Uses `hookSpecificOutput` for richer control: allow, deny, or escalate to the user. You can also modify tool input before it runs or inject additional context for Claude. See [PreToolUse decision control](#pretooluse-decision-control) for the full set of options.
   239→529-   529→
   240→530-   530→    ```json  theme={null}
   241→531-   531→    {
   242→532-   532→      "hookSpecificOutput": {
   243→533:   533→        "hookEventName": "PreToolUse",
   244→534-   534→        "permissionDecision": "deny",
   245→535-   535→        "permissionDecisionReason": "Database writes are not allowed"
   246→536-   536→      }
   247→537-   537→    }
   248→538-   538→    ```
   249→--
   250→708-   708→<Note>
   251→709-   709→  The JSON format isn't required for simple use cases. To add context, you can print plain text to stdout with exit code 0. Use JSON when you need to
   252→710-   710→  block prompts or want more structured control.
   253→711-   711→</Note>
   254→712-   712→
   255→713:   713→### PreToolUse
   256→714-   714→
   257→715-   715→Runs after Claude creates tool parameters and before processing the tool call. Matches on tool name: `Bash`, `Edit`, `Write`, `Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`, `Task`, `WebFetch`, `WebSearch`, and any [MCP tool names](#match-mcp-tools).
   258→716-   716→
   259→717:   717→Use [PreToolUse decision control](#pretooluse-decision-control) to allow, deny, or ask for permission to use the tool.
   260→718-   718→
   261→719:   719→#### PreToolUse input
   262→720-   720→
   263→721:   721→In addition to the [common input fields](#common-input-fields), PreToolUse hooks receive `tool_name`, `tool_input`, and `tool_use_id`. The `tool_input` fields depend on the tool:
   264→722-   722→
   265→723-   723→##### Bash
   266→724-   724→
   267→725-   725→Executes shell commands.
   268→726-   726→
   269→--
   270→811-   811→| `prompt`        | string | `"Find all API endpoints"` | The task for the agent to perform            |
   271→812-   812→| `description`   | string | `"Find API endpoints"`     | Short description of the task                |
   272→813-   813→| `subagent_type` | string | `"Explore"`                | Type of specialized agent to use             |
   273→814-   814→| `model`         | string | `"sonnet"`                 | Optional model alias to override the default |
   274→815-   815→
   275→816:   816→#### PreToolUse decision control
   276→817-   817→
   277→818:   818→`PreToolUse` hooks can control whether a tool call proceeds. Unlike other hooks that use a top-level `decision` field, PreToolUse returns its decision inside a `hookSpecificOutput` object. This gives it richer control: three outcomes (allow, deny, or ask) plus the ability to modify tool input before execution.
   278→819-   819→
   279→820-   820→| Field                      | Description                                                                                                                                      |
   280→821-   821→| :------------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
   281→822-   822→| `permissionDecision`       | `"allow"` bypasses the permission system, `"deny"` prevents the tool call, `"ask"` prompts the user to confirm                                   |
   282→823-   823→| `permissionDecisionReason` | For `"allow"` and `"ask"`, shown to the user but not Claude. For `"deny"`, shown to Claude                                                       |
   283→--
   284→825-   825→| `additionalContext`        | String added to Claude's context before the tool executes                                                                                        |
   285→826-   826→
   286→827-   827→```json  theme={null}
   287→828-   828→{
   288→829-   829→  "hookSpecificOutput": {
   289→830:   830→    "hookEventName": "PreToolUse",
   290→831-   831→    "permissionDecision": "allow",
   291→832-   832→    "permissionDecisionReason": "My reason here",
   292→833-   833→    "updatedInput": {
   293→834-   834→      "field_to_modify": "new value"
   294→835-   835→    },
   295→--
   296→837-   837→  }
   297→838-   838→}
   298→839-   839→```
   299→840-   840→
   300→841-   841→<Note>
   301→842:   842→  PreToolUse previously used top-level `decision` and `reason` fields, but these are deprecated for this event. Use `hookSpecificOutput.permissionDecision` and `hookSpecificOutput.permissionDecisionReason` instead. The deprecated values `"approve"` and `"block"` map to `"allow"` and `"deny"` respectively. Other events like PostToolUse and Stop continue to use top-level `decision` and `reason` as their current format.
   302→843-   843→</Note>
   303→844-   844→
   304→845-   845→### PermissionRequest
   305→846-   846→
   306→847-   847→Runs when the user is shown a permission dialog.
   307→848-   848→Use [PermissionRequest decision control](#permissionrequest-decision-control) to allow or deny on behalf of the user.
   308→849-   849→
   309→850:   850→Matches on tool name, same values as PreToolUse.
   310→851-   851→
   311→852-   852→#### PermissionRequest input
   312→853-   853→
   313→854:   854→PermissionRequest hooks receive `tool_name` and `tool_input` fields like PreToolUse hooks, but without `tool_use_id`. An optional `permission_suggestions` array contains the "always allow" options the user would normally see in the permission dialog. The difference is when the hook fires: PermissionRequest hooks run when a permission dialog is about to be shown to the user, while PreToolUse hooks run before tool execution regardless of permission status.
   314→855-   855→
   315→856-   856→```json  theme={null}
   316→857-   857→{
   317→858-   858→  "session_id": "abc123",
   318→859-   859→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
   319→--
   320→899-   899→
   321→900-   900→### PostToolUse
   322→901-   901→
   323→902-   902→Runs immediately after a tool completes successfully.
   324→903-   903→
   325→904:   904→Matches on tool name, same values as PreToolUse.
   326→905-   905→
   327→906-   906→#### PostToolUse input
   328→907-   907→
   329→908-   908→`PostToolUse` hooks fire after a tool has already executed successfully. The input includes both `tool_input`, the arguments sent to the tool, and `tool_response`, the result it returned. The exact schema for both depends on the tool.
   330→909-   909→
   331→--
   332→951-   951→
   333→952-   952→### PostToolUseFailure
   334→953-   953→
   335→954-   954→Runs when a tool execution fails. This event fires for tool calls that throw errors or return failure results. Use this to log failures, send alerts, or provide corrective feedback to Claude.
   336→955-   955→
   337→956:   956→Matches on tool name, same values as PreToolUse.
   338→957-   957→
   339→958-   958→#### PostToolUseFailure input
   340→959-   959→
   341→960-   960→PostToolUseFailure hooks receive the same `tool_name` and `tool_input` fields as PostToolUse, along with error information as top-level fields:
   342→961-   961→
   343→--
   344→1253-  1253→
   345→1254-  1254→The matcher filters on the configuration source:
   346→1255-  1255→
   347→1256-  1256→| Matcher            | When it fires                             |
   348→1257-  1257→| :----------------- | :---------------------------------------- |
   349→1258:  1258→| `user_settings`    | `~/.claude/settings.json` changes         |
   350→1259:  1259→| `project_settings` | `.claude/settings.json` changes           |
   351→1260-  1260→| `local_settings`   | `.claude/settings.local.json` changes     |
   352→1261-  1261→| `policy_settings`  | Managed policy settings change            |
   353→1262-  1262→| `skills`           | A skill file in `.claude/skills/` changes |
   354→1263-  1263→
   355→1264-  1264→This example logs all configuration changes for security auditing:
   356→--
   357→1290-  1290→  "transcript_path": "/Users/.../.claude/projects/.../00893aaf-19fa-41d2-8238-13269b9b3ca0.jsonl",
   358→1291-  1291→  "cwd": "/Users/...",
   359→1292-  1292→  "permission_mode": "default",
   360→1293-  1293→  "hook_event_name": "ConfigChange",
   361→1294-  1294→  "source": "project_settings",
   362→1295:  1295→  "file_path": "/Users/.../my-project/.claude/settings.json"
   363→1296-  1296→}
   364→1297-  1297→```
   365→1298-  1298→
   366→1299-  1299→#### ConfigChange decision control
   367→1300-  1300→
   368→--
   369→1466-  1466→Events that support all three hook types (`command`, `prompt`, and `agent`):
   370→1467-  1467→
   371→1468-  1468→* `PermissionRequest`
   372→1469-  1469→* `PostToolUse`
   373→1470-  1470→* `PostToolUseFailure`
   374→1471:  1471→* `PreToolUse`
   375→1472-  1472→* `Stop`
   376→1473-  1473→* `SubagentStop`
   377→1474-  1474→* `TaskCompleted`
   378→1475-  1475→* `UserPromptSubmit`
   379→1476-  1476→
   380→--
   381→1674-  1674→else
   382→1675-  1675→  echo "{\"systemMessage\": \"Tests failed after editing $FILE_PATH: $RESULT\"}"
   383→1676-  1676→fi
   384→1677-  1677→```
   385→1678-  1678→
   386→1679:  1679→Then add this configuration to `.claude/settings.json` in your project root. The `async: true` flag lets Claude keep working while tests run:
   387→1680-  1680→
   388→1681-  1681→```json  theme={null}
   389→1682-  1682→{
   390→1683-  1683→  "hooks": {
   391→1684-  1684→    "PostToolUse": [
   392→--
   393→1702-  1702→
   394→1703-  1703→Async hooks have several constraints compared to synchronous hooks:
   395→1704-  1704→
   396→1705-  1705→* Only `type: "command"` hooks support `async`. Prompt-based hooks cannot run asynchronously.
   397→1706-  1706→* Async hooks cannot block tool calls or return decisions. By the time the hook completes, the triggering action has already proceeded.
   398→1707:  1707→* Hook output is delivered on the next conversation turn. If the session is idle, the response waits until the next user interaction.
   399→1708-  1708→* Each execution creates a separate background process. There is no deduplication across multiple firings of the same async hook.
   400→1709-  1709→
   401→1710-  1710→## Security considerations
   402→1711-  1711→

   651→   651→Any variables written to this file will be available in all subsequent Bash commands that Claude Code executes during the session.
   652→   652→
   653→   653→<Note>
   654→   654→  `CLAUDE_ENV_FILE` is available for SessionStart hooks. Other hook types do not have access to this variable.
